library("additivityTests")
library("gplots")
library("plyr")

21.7

21.7.a

Age is used as a blocking variable because age is a highly relevant metabolic covariate. That is, peoples’ metabolisms slow as they age.

21.7.b

# Load data
fat = c(.73, .86, .94, 1.40, 1.62,
        .67, .75, .81, 1.32, 1.41,
        .15, .21, .26, .75, .78)
df = data.frame(fat)
age = c()
diet = c()
for (i in 1:length(fat)){
    age = c(age, ((i - 1) %% 5) + 1)
    diet = c(diet, ceiling(i / 5))
}
df$age = as.factor(age)
df$diet = as.factor(diet)

aov_fat = with(df, aov(fat ~ diet + age))
anova(aov_fat)
## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Response: fat
##           Df  Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
## diet       2 1.32028 0.66014  273.35 4.326e-08 ***
## age        4 1.41896 0.35474  146.89 1.610e-07 ***
## Residuals  8 0.01932 0.00242                      
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

We see that both age and diet are significant drivers of lipid content.

par(mfrow=c(2,2))
plot(aov_fat)

Notice that the residuals are not normal and heteroskedastic. We interpret the difference between the emperical to theoretical quantiles in the QQ plot to entail the invalidity of the normality asumption. We interpret the non-horizontal red line in the residuals plot to entail heteroskedacity.

21.7.c

with(aov_fat, interaction.plot(age, diet, fat))

The parallell lines in the interaction plot suggest that there are no interactions present between the age and diet factors on the lipid content response.

21.7.d

alp = 0.05
df_m = matrix(df$fat, 5,3)
tt = tukey.test(df_m, alpha = alp)
pv = df(tt$stat, 1, 15-5-3)

The model under consideration is \(Y_{i,j} = \alpha_i + \beta_j + \hat{D}\alpha_i\beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}\). If \(\frac{\hat{D}\sum\sum\alpha_i\beta_j}{\frac{SSR}{ab-a-b}} > F^{*}\) where \(F^{*} = F_{1,ab-a-b}(1-\frac{0.05}{2})\), then we reject the null hypothesis that there is a non-zero Tukey-type additive interaction between diet and age. The P-value is \(0.0111415 <0.05\) so we reject the additive (null) hypothesis.

21.8

21.8.a

anova(aov_fat)
## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Response: fat
##           Df  Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
## diet       2 1.32028 0.66014  273.35 4.326e-08 ***
## age        4 1.41896 0.35474  146.89 1.610e-07 ***
## Residuals  8 0.01932 0.00242                      
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

21.8.b

with(df, plotmeans(fat~diet,
                xlab="Diet type",
                ylab="Lipid content",
                main="Mean Plot\nwith 95% CI"))

It appears that the 3rd diet type is sigificantly different from the first two diet types, but the first two are not significantly different from each other.

21.8.c

a1 = with(df, aov(fat ~ diet))
tuk = TukeyHSD(a1)
tuk
##   Tukey multiple comparisons of means
##     95% family-wise confidence level
## 
## Fit: aov(formula = fat ~ diet)
## 
## $diet
##       diff        lwr         upr     p adj
## 2-1 -0.118 -0.7021505  0.46615046 0.8539224
## 3-1 -0.680 -1.2641505 -0.09584954 0.0229423
## 3-2 -0.562 -1.1461505  0.02215046 0.0596875

Indeed it appears that diet 3 is different from 1 and 2, but 1 and 2 are not significantly different.

21.8.d

alp = 0.05
u12 = -tuk$diet[1]
u23 = -tuk$diet[3]
n = dim(df)[1]
a = length(unique(df$diet))
b = length(unique(df$age))
ci_add = qt(1-alp/4, n-(a+b-1)) * sqrt(anova(aov_fat)["diet", "Mean Sq"])
u12.uci = u12 + ci_add
u12.lci = u12 - ci_add
u23.uci = u23 + ci_add
u23.lci = u23 - ci_add

The estimates of \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) are \(\hat{L_1} = \hat{\mu_1} - \hat{\mu_2} = 0.118 \pm 2.2355854\) and \(\hat{L_2} = \hat{\mu_2} - \hat{\mu_3} = 0.562 \pm 2.2355854\). It appears that, using the Bonferoni correction for multiple comparisons at a confidence level of \(\alpha = 0.95\), there are not significant differences between the means of the diet treatments examined.

21.8.e

a2 = with(df, aov(fat ~ age))
tuk2 = TukeyHSD(a2)
tuk2
##   Tukey multiple comparisons of means
##     95% family-wise confidence level
## 
## Fit: aov(formula = fat ~ age)
## 
## $age
##           diff        lwr      upr     p adj
## 2-1 0.09000000 -0.8935143 1.073514 0.9978910
## 3-1 0.15333333 -0.8301809 1.136848 0.9840614
## 4-1 0.64000000 -0.3435143 1.623514 0.2754096
## 5-1 0.75333333 -0.2301809 1.736848 0.1614353
## 3-2 0.06333333 -0.9201809 1.046848 0.9994673
## 4-2 0.55000000 -0.4335143 1.533514 0.4040709
## 5-2 0.66333333 -0.3201809 1.646848 0.2477021
## 4-3 0.48666667 -0.4968476 1.470181 0.5132985
## 5-3 0.60000000 -0.3835143 1.583514 0.3283526
## 5-4 0.11333333 -0.8701809 1.096848 0.9948734

It appears that age block 1 might be different from 4 and 5, but there is little evidence for this claim. More replications within each block would support this claim.

21.8.f

The lack of a control diet in this study indicates that the researchers are not interested in deviations in blood lipid content between treatment and typical diets. There may have been budget constraints, they also may already have a good sense of the effects of age on blood lipid content for a standard (control) diet. Regardless, it’s not invalid to be curious about differences between treatments.

21.15

sig = 0.04
u1 = 1.1
u2 = 1.0
u3 = .9
u = (u1 + u2 + u3) / 3
n = 15
a = 3
b = 5
alp = 0.05
mstr = (u1 - u)^2 + (u2 - u)^2 +(u3 - u)^2
empChiStat = mstr / sig^2
degfred = n - a - 1
theorChiStat = qchisq(1-alp, degfred)

We see that the theoretical \(\chi^2_{0.05, 11} = 19.6751376\) and the emperical \(\hat{\chi^2} = 12.5\). This evidence suggests that the samples were likely drawn from a single normal disribution, indicating that treatment effects are not likely present. The \(\chi^2\) test is used because the sample statistic is a normalized sum of squared random variables - that the test statistic is within reasonable bounds for such a distribution represented theoretically indicates that the random variables in question may very well be standard normal as per the definition of the \(\chi^2_d = \sum_{i=1}^{d} Z_i^2\) for iid \(Z_i\sim N(0,1)\).

TODO

21.17

21.19

n = 5
r = 3
us = ddply(df,~age,summarise,mean=mean(fat))$mean
u = mean(df$fat)
msbltr = sum((u-df$fat)^2) / (n*r)
msbl = sum((u-us)^2) / n
eh = ((n-1)*msbl + n*(r-1)*msbltr) / ((n*r-1)*msbltr)

The efficiency measure (12.14) is \(\hat{E}=0.8612527\) so we need about \(0.8612527\) times as many replicates per treatment to achieve a similar variance within contrasts if we had not blocked. In this case, blocking was inefficient.
